claimText: Altermagnets exhibit alternating spins and possess no net magnetism, a characteristic they share with antiferromagnets.
evidenceExcerpt: Like antiferromagnets, they exhibit alternating spins and therefore have no net magnetism.
examUse: Statement-based question on the fundamental properties of altermagnets.
distractorHooks: Claiming altermagnets have a strong net magnetism
Confusing their spin structure with ferromagnets

claimText: Due to their unique crystal symmetry, altermagnets display intense internal spin polarisation similar to ferromagnets.
evidenceExcerpt: But due to their unique crystal symmetry, they show intense internal spin polarisation, similar to ferromagnets.
examUse: Statement-based question comparing altermagnets to ferromagnets.
distractorHooks: Attributing spin polarisation to external magnetic fields instead of crystal symmetry
Stating altermagnets lack internal spin polarisation

claimText: Chromium antimonide (CrSb) is an altermagnetic material that exhibits the strongest spin-splitting among known altermagnets.
evidenceExcerpt: CrSb exhibits the strongest spin-splitting among known altermagnets.
examUse: Fact-based statement or correct-pair question identifying specific altermagnetic materials.
distractorHooks: Naming a different compound as the strongest altermagnet
Stating CrSb has weak or no spin-splitting

claimText: Scientists from the S.N. Bose National Centre for Basic Sciences discovered the altermagnetic properties using chromium antimonide (CrSb).
evidenceExcerpt: Discovered by scientists from S.N. Bose National Centre for Basic Sciences using chromium antimonide (CrSb).
examUse: Institution-feature question regarding recent scientific discoveries in India.
distractorHooks: Attributing the discovery to IISc, ISRO, or DRDO
Claiming the discovery was made using a traditional ferromagnetic material
